pattern: Recognize that counting index-ordered pairs whose product equals a given square is best solved by hashing all pairwise products once, then looking each square up.
hints: Checking every (i, j, k) triple directly works but is far too slow at the input limits; you need to avoid recomputing pair products from scratch for each source-array element.
Precompute every ordered-index pair product from one array into a frequency map, then for each element of the other array just look up whether its square appears as a key.
Run this lookup process twice — once treating nums1 as the 'squares' array and nums2 as the 'pairs' array, and once with the roles swapped — then sum both counts.

edge_inputs.description: single-element arrays: no pair is possible in either direction
duplicate value shared across arrays produces a matching pair
repeated identical values in both arrays: multiple pairs share the same product
a pair of equal values whose product equals the square (self-pair via two equal entries)
nums2 too short to ever form a pair (length 1), only type-2 triplets possible
no triplet exists in either direction

stress.description: two mid-sized arrays over a small value range to force many coincidental matching products
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n1 = rng.randint(1, min(size, 200))
    n2 = rng.randint(1, min(size, 200))
    nums1 = [rng.randint(1, 50) for _ in range(n1)]
    nums2 = [rng.randint(1, 50) for _ in range(n2)]
    return [nums1, nums2]
stress.note: small value range (1..50) keeps arrays dense with matching products while staying well within length limits
